metadata.title: Can incivility be informative? Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.

abstract: Workplace incivility is generally viewed as a deleterious interpersonal stressor. Yet, alternative theories suggest that incivility may have instrumental implications for some targets. Applying signaling theory, we study client-provider relationships in a health care context to unpack linkages between incivility enacted by organizational outsiders and work creativity responses by employee targets. We argue that providers leverage information from client incivility to provide more creative care over time. In Study 1 (N = 186), results suggest that clients may use incivility to signal perceptions of poor treatment quality to providers. In Study 2 (N = 416), results from topic modeling of qualitative data show that providers observe client incivility and believe it can contain valuable information about client satisfaction. In Study 3 (N = 503), providers reported their experiences of client incivility and creativity (incremental and radical) in client care over five waves of data to capture the incubation time that providers may need to reflect on instances of incivility. Employing trait-state-occasion modeling, our findings show that episodic (i.e., higher than normal) client incivility had positive lagged relationships with incremental and radical provider creativity, suggesting that time is needed for providers to process the information contained in the client incivility signal and creatively modify treatment plans. Theoretical and practical implications for workplace incivility and creativity are discussed.

paragraphs: Rudeness abounds in today's workplace. Of the many ways people are mistreated at work, workplace incivility is differentiated as a distinct construct by its ambiguous intent to harm (Andersson & Pearson, 1999;Yao et al., 2021). Examples of incivility include giving the silent treatment, interrupting colleagues, and making jokes at others' expense (Matthews & Ritter, 2016). The more subtle, ambiguous nature of incivility should not be mistaken for triviality. Meta-analytic work shows that incivility is as damaging as higher-intensity mistreatment, even workplace bullying (Hershcovis, 2011), and incivility stifles employee performance and creativity (Porath & Erez, 2007, 2009). Recently, though, researchers have suggested that incivility may have instrumental value (Cortina et al., 2017). The proposed instrumentality of incivility rests on the idea that it may have an informational component and that the information conveyed by uncivil actions could drive positive behavior change in the target (Miner et al., 2018). Although intriguing, research is needed to test such a contentious proposition.

We address this gap by leveraging a novel theory in the mistreatment literature, signaling theory (Connelly et al., 2011;Spence, 1973), which suggests that people seek new, relevant information from others (i.e., signals) to help make important decisions. Despite the negative implications of incivility for employee well-being (Lim & Cortina, 2005), incivility may also provide a useful signal of the enactor's disagreement or discontent with the target (Kim & Kim, 2020)-akin to negative feedback. In line with evidence that negative feedback can produce positive behavioral effects (e.g., Kluger & DeNisi, 1996), we suggest that incivility may signal a need for positive behavior change in the target, specifically in the form of employee creativity. Such possibilities represent a profound shift in our understanding of incivility's impact.

To be clear, we suggest that the instrumental outcomes of workplace incivility are likely confined to specific contexts, relationships, and theoretically relevant constructs (Johns, 2006;Schilpzand et al., 2016). We assert that examining incivility within the relationships an individual has with organizational outsiders (i.e., customers, clients) may be particularly useful to understanding potential instrumental effects. Existing research suggests that workplace mistreatment functions differently depending on the perpetrator (Hershcovis & Barling, 2010;Sliter et al., 2012). Conceptual arguments (Schilpzand et al., 2016) and a meta-analysis examining workplace aggression (Hershcovis & Barling, 2010) imply that incivility enacted by organizational insiders (i.e., supervisors, coworkers) is more damaging than that enacted by outsiders, likely because the target may more explicitly fault the organization for insider-enacted incivility. We draw from this evidence to position employee/outsider relationships as a relational context in which the instrumental impact of incivility on employee behavior may be likely to manifest. Across three studies, we collect data from health care clients (Study 1) and providers (Studies 2 and 3), as client/ provider relationships are an incredibly common (Bureau of Labor Statistics, 2019a) and societally significant (American Occupational Therapy Association, Inc [AOTA], 2019a; American Physical Therapy Association [APTA], 2019;BLS, 2019bBLS, , 2019c) ) employee/outsider relationship. Because providers develop meaningful relationships with their clients (e.g., Palmadottir, 2006), providers may be especially sensitive to client behaviors that communicate information about the quality of their experience, including client incivility.

We propose that employee creativity is a theoretically relevant construct that may relate to outsider (i.e., client) incivility in this way. We define client incivility as low-intensity deviant behavior with ambiguous intent to harm, which providers may experience from their clients (Andersson & Pearson, 1999). Moreover, we define provider creativity as the production of novel and practical treatment options (Amabile et al., 1996). Emerging research in the creativity literature suggests that negative bottom-up feedback can drive creativity (Kim & Kim, 2020), and client incivility may be one implicit way that such feedback manifests. Conceptually, a provider may interpret client incivility as a sign of discontent and react by creatively making changes in the way they do their job. Grounded in signaling theory, we view client incivility as observable behavior that contains valuable information for providers which may inform their decision-making with respect to client care. To provide quality care, providers must understand the effects of their treatment plans, leading them to search for signals about their clients' experiences. We propose that client incivility acts as one useful signal. For example, a client may make a rude comment to the provider when a session is going poorly, thereby signaling their discontent with the current care. Our working premise is that when providers experience client incivility, they can leverage this information to be more creative in their approach to client care over time by modifying treatment or therapy regimens. Such changes may manifest as increased creativity in care delivered, which is beneficial to both client and provider. Indeed, creativity is essential for effective job performance in numerous health care professions (The Lancet, 2006), including the groups sampled in the present research (i.e., physical and occupational therapists, National Center for O * NET Development, 2019aDevelopment, , 2019b)).

We make four contributions to the workplace incivility and creativity literatures. First, previous research has generally viewed incivility as an interpersonal stressor (Cortina et al., 2017), and uniformly dysfunctional (Miner et al., 2018). We attempt to shift the conversation by studying workplace incivility through a signaling theory lens and demonstrate the utility of alternative perspectives, which acknowledge the potential instrumental outcomes of incivility. Second, we propose that the relationship between incivility and creativity is more complex than is suggested in prior research. Our arguments contrast the extant literature which has consistently observed negative relationships between incivility and creativity (Porath & Erez, 2007, 2009;Yip et al., 2018;Zhan et al., 2019). We maintain that incivility and creativity have a more complex temporal relationship than is currently theorized.

Third, most mistreatment research uses cross-sectional designs, which are insufficient for understanding incivility (Su et al., 2021) and testing signaling theory, which inherently captures temporal dynamics (Connelly et al., 2011). Scholarship on the incivilitycreativity relation is similarly limited by the lack of diversity in methods used by researchers. Previous experimental work provides valuable insight into the more immediate link between incivility and creativity (Porath & Erez, 2007, 2009;Yip et al., 2018), but additional research is needed that focuses on increased external validity and over-time associations. To address these issues, we conducted three field studies, leveraging different samples and methodologies to gain insights into relationships between client incivility and provider creativity.

Fourth, we contribute to the creativity literature by examining how fluctuations in creativity may reciprocally result in client incivility, based on the concept of countersignaling (Connelly et al., 2011). In this aim, we also address calls to study antecedents to incivility (Schilpzand et al., 2016) and build on a literature in which creativity is generally positioned as a behavioral outcome of incivility (Porath & Erez, 2007, 2009;Yip et al., 2018). We also leverage an emerging creativity taxonomy (i.e., incremental vs. radical; Gilson & Madjar, 2011) to examine whether the incivilitycreativity relation differs by creativity type.

sections.header_raw: Existing Theory Positions Incivility as a Deleterious Stressor
sections.header_norm: existing theory positions incivility as a deleterious stressor
paragraphs: Incivility is typically, and justifiably, positioned as harmful. Of the theories scholars draw upon to explain target's reactions and responses to incivility at work (see Schilpzand et al., 2016), most lead researchers to conceptualize incivility as a stressor that induces strain and negative behaviors in targets. For example, applications of Hobfoll's (1989) conservation of resources theory frame incivility as a detrimental stressor that can deplete worker's valued resources, ultimately leading to strain outcomes such as increased burnout (Taylor et al., 2017). Scholars have also utilized Weiss and Cropanzano's (1996) affective events theory, positioning incivility as a negative workplace event that drives negative emotional reactions and thus harms targets (e.g., Bunk & Magley, 2013). Moreover, since Andersson and Pearson's (1999) seminal publication and their proposition of "tit for tat" incivility spirals, researchers have also viewed incivility within a social interactionist framework by drawing upon theories of social exchange and reciprocity (e.g., Blau, 1964;Gouldner, 1960). This perspective suggests that targets of incivility are likely to become perpetrators of incivility and other forms of mistreatment (Gallus et al., 2014;Taylor & Kluemper, 2012). Likewise, even when researchers apply theories that acknowledge the ambiguity of potential stressors and the importance of personal appraisal in determining outcomes (e.g., the transactional model of stress; Lazarus & Folkman, 1984), most evidence suggests that targets appraise incivility negatively (Cortina & Magley, 2009) and thus experience negative outcomes. With these common theories in mind, it is understandable that researchers have hypothesized and observed negative relationships between incivility and creativity (Porath & Erez, 2007, 2009;Yip et al., 2018;Zhan et al., 2019). Incivility is thought to undermine creativity because it distracts employees from creative work tasks and decreases motivation to work toward creative work goals.

We believe there are several reasons why a positive, instrumental relation between incivility and creativity has not yet been observed. First, stress frameworks provide limited potential to explain the potential informational and instrumental effects of incivility. Novel theory is needed to guide hypotheses regarding the potential This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

instrumentality of incivility. Second, context and timing are central to understanding employee creativity (Kim & Kim, 2020;van Knippenberg & Hirst, 2015). But these features have not been considered in studying incivility and creativity in the workplace. Applied here, we do not argue that incivility always sparks a creative response by the target, as the aforementioned theory and research clearly indicate that negative consequences are common. Instead, we expect that client incivility may incite a lagged creativity response by a health care provider. Appropriate study design is also needed to attend to context and timing in this way. Namely, longitudinal field studies are needed to capture employee creativity with greater external and construct validity (Amabile, 1996;Zhou & Shalley, 2011). Such designs may also better capture incivility as an experience that occurs frequently (i.e., sometimes multiple times in 1 day; Zhou & Hoever, 2014), occurs within ongoing relationships rather than with strangers (e.g., Ferguson, 2012), and encompasses a variety of behaviors (Cortina et al., 2013).

But these features have not been considered in studying incivility and creativity in the workplace. Applied here, we do not argue that incivility always sparks a creative response by the target, as the aforementioned theory and research clearly indicate that negative consequences are common. Instead, we expect that client incivility may incite a lagged creativity response by a health care provider. Appropriate study design is also needed to attend to context and timing in this way. Namely, longitudinal field studies are needed to capture employee creativity with greater external and construct validity (Amabile, 1996;Zhou & Shalley, 2011). Such designs may also better capture incivility as an experience that occurs frequently (i.e., sometimes multiple times in 1 day; Zhou & Hoever, 2014), occurs within ongoing relationships rather than with strangers (e.g., Ferguson, 2012), and encompasses a variety of behaviors (Cortina et al., 2013).
sections.preview_head: Incivility is typically, and justifiably, positioned as harmful. Of the theories scholars draw upon to explain target's reactions and responses to incivility at work (see Schilpzand et al., 2016), most lead researchers to conceptualize incivility as a stressor that induces strain and negative behaviors in targets. For example, applications of Hobfoll's (1989) conservation of resources theory frame incivility as a detrimental stressor that can deplete worker's valued resources, ultimately leading to strain outcomes such as increased burnout (Taylor et al., 2017).

sections.paper_id: Matthews et al. - 2022 - Can incivility be informative Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.
sections.header_raw: Signaling Theory Positions Client Incivility as an Instrumental Signal
sections.header_norm: signaling theory positions client incivility as an instrumental signal
paragraphs: We apply signaling theory to suggest that a positive relationship between incivility and creativity may exist over time and in the context of client-provider relationships. We provide an overview of signaling theory to explain this premise. Signaling theorists emphasize information asymmetry and the role it plays in decision-making (Connelly et al., 2011). Information asymmetry exists when one entity (e.g., person) holds information that another entity might value in their decision-making. As an example, signaling theory is used to understand the hiring process because information asymmetry exists between applicants and hiring organizations (Suazo et al., 2009). Each entity has information about their own qualities that are not observable to the other entity but may be relevant to the decisions at hand. An entity may send observable signals about their unobservable quality to enhance their likelihood of selection by the other entity, and interpret signals to make decisions about where to work (applicants) and whom to hire (hiring organizations).

Information asymmetry also manifests between clients and providers. Each entity possesses information about treatment that they may signal to communicate about and potentially improve the quality of care. Specifically, clients have information about the effect the care has on them (i.e., an unobservable quality), and the provider needs this information-in the form of an observable signal-to inform their decision-making with regard to the provision of optimal care, to encourage compliance, and to promote overall service satisfaction (Hall et al., 2010;Muir, 2012). Qualitative research indicates that outsider incivility toward an employee is often driven by the outsider's discontent with their interaction with the employee (Sliter & Jones, 2016). Client incivility, then, is a kind of need signal which "communicates requirements to the receiver" (Connelly et al., 2011, p. 60) by observably signaling the otherwise unobservable quality of their own dissatisfaction with the current treatment approach. The provider, the receiver of this need signal, may then use this new information about the clients' discontent to creatively improve care, given that their job inherently requires active listening to all types of client feedback to thoughtfully improve treatment using creative adaptation and problem solving (National Center for O * NET Development, 2019a, 2019b). Of note, there is a signaling timeline, or an underlying temporal process.

First, clients must perceive some form of poor treatment on the part of the provider; in turn, clients must engage in some form of incivility and, in doing so, signal information about their experience to providers. From there, it takes time for providers to observe, interpret, and act upon this information.

Incivility is, of course, only one way that a client can send their provider a signal about treatment satisfaction. For example, through informal conversations and formal assessments (e.g., of standard clinical outcomes), providers can solicit information (e.g., both positive and negative feedback) from clients to inform treatment plans (AOTA, 2019b;Jette et al., 2009). Traditionally, effective signals are those that positively highlight the quality of an entity (e.g., a hirable trait in a job applicant, a satisfactory aspect of care), thereby differentiating that entity from others and facilitating their selection by decision-makers (Connelly et al., 2011;Spence, 1973). However, this need not always be the case. For example, Hillman et al. (2011) studied negative signals, in particular how shareholders signal their discontent with publicly traded firms and their director nominees. Models of interpersonal conflict further classify expressions of discontent based on their intensity and clarity (Weingart et al., 2015). For instance, a client could directly communicate negative feedback to a provider (clear but not intense), enact verbal or physical abuse (clear and intense), or act uncivilly when experiencing discontent (ambiguous and passive).

Regarding the particular connection between incivility and creativity, conflict researchers argue that passive, ambiguous expression of discontent leaves room for subjective interpretation by the receiver as to how to appropriately respond (Weingart et al., 2015). Indirect cues seem to provide broad information that suggests continued refinements and changes to employee behavior are needed while also failing to specify the nature of such changes, thus inciting greater creativity (De Stobbeleir et al., 2011). Client incivility may thus signal some degree of treatment discontent to a provider, motivating creative attempts to improve treatment in the absence of explicit suggestions.

sections.paper_id: Matthews et al. - 2022 - Can incivility be informative Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.
paragraphs: To ensure a comprehensive test of underlying signaling principles, we conducted three studies to test our assumptions pertaining to the processes under consideration. In Study 1, we collected data from clients to establish whether there is a relation between clients' discontent with treatment (an unobservable quality) and their enacted uncivil behavior directed at the provider (an observable signal of discontent). In Study 2, we sought to identify a baseline understanding of providers' experience of client incivility. Specifically, we examined the degree to which providers actually report experiencing client incivility, which taps into the observability of client incivility as a signal (i.e., signal observability), as well as the degree to which providers pay attention to client incivility as a meaningful signal (i.e., receiver attention), both of which are required for a behavioral reaction by the receiver to occur. Finally, in Study 3, we examined dynamic relations between providers' experiences of client incivility and their own creativity over time. As such, Study 1 seeks to establish whether organizational outsiders use incivility to signal discontent, Study 2 delves into whether employees observe and attend to the signal, and Study 3 clarifies the timeline and nature of creativity as a behavioral reaction to the This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

sections.paper_id: Matthews et al. - 2022 - Can incivility be informative Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.
paragraphs: In Study 1, we focused on the outsider (i.e., client) perspective to test whether client incivility is an honest signal (Durcikova & Gray, 2009). Signal honesty is defined "as the extent to which the signaler actually has the underlying quality associated with the signal" (Connelly et al., 2011, p. 46). Specific to our study, clients are honest signalers if they hold negative perceptions of the quality of care, and incivility directed at providers functions is a signal of such perceptions. We focus on two indicators of perceived quality of care: (a) perceptions of provider involvement (i.e., whether clients perceive providers as actively engaged in a positive information exchange about provided care; Smith et al., 2006) and (b) provider satisfaction (i.e., how clients evaluate the overall quality of care based on provider behaviors; Hsieh, 2017). If clients are honest signalers, then these indicators should relate negatively to client incivility, such that clients engage in higher levels of incivility when they perceive lower quality care.
Research Question 1: Are clients honest signalers, such that client perceptions of provider involvement and provider satisfaction negatively relate to client incivility?

sections.paper_id: Matthews et al. - 2022 - Can incivility be informative Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.
paragraphs: Participants and Procedure Amazon.com's Mechanical Turk (MTurk) was used to recruit a sample of clients currently in a client-provider relationship. Metaanalytic work shows that effect sizes in data collected via MTurk are comparable to other survey methods (Walter et al., 2019). In addition, MTurk provided an opportunity for us to study clients in an active relationship with a provider (i.e., an occupational therapist, physical therapist or therapist assistant) without jeopardizing their confidentiality as clients. Confidentiality in this instance is not only in line with health care provider ethics but has been shown to improve accuracy of self-reports of counterproductive work behaviors (Berry et al., 2012), such as incivility. Participants were screened to ensure they were in an active relationship, such that they had seen a provider at least once in the past 6 months; 1,023 respondents were screened, identifying 201 in an active relationship. Participants were compensated $1.50. However, 15 respondents failed two or more of the five attention checks used to ensure effortful responding (i.e., "Leave this question blank") and were excluded from our sample. Within the analysis sample (N = 186), 41.4% were female, the majority (80.6%) were between the ages of 25 and 44, and 78.0% of the sample had been seeing their therapist for at least 3 months.

paragraphs: Client incivility (α = .98) was assessed with an adapted version of Cortina et al.'s (2013) 12-item measure. Instructions were, "Thinking about the last several sessions you had with this health care provider, how often did you engage in any of these behaviors?" Responses were made on a 5-point frequency scale (1 = Never to 5 = Many times). A sample item is, "Ignored him/her or failed to speak to him/her (e.g., gave him/her "the silent treatment")."

paragraphs: Provider involvement (α = .89) was assessed with six items (Smith et al., 2006). Instructions were, "Thinking about this specific health care provider, please answer the following." A sample item is, "I find it hard to talk with my provider because he/she is always in such a hurry." Items were reverse-coded as necessary so higher scores reflect more involvement. sections.paper_id: Matthews et al. - 2022 - Can incivility be informative Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.
paragraphs: Although we believe that clients are honest signalers (i.e., they use incivility to convey information about the quality of care received), distortions may disrupt the quality of information being conveyed (Connelly et al., 2011). Consequently, we accounted for potential internal drivers of client incivility which may create distortion in signaling discontent. Given the physical discomfort clients may experience, they may have reduced ability to selfregulate their behavior, which could lead them to engage in incivility (Meier & Gross, 2015). Past research also suggests that individuals in more negative mood states may be more likely to engage in varying types of interpersonal aggression (Hershcovis et al., 2007). Thus, we included two control variables in the analysis. Client somatic strain (α = .76) was assessed with four items (Pejtersen et al., 2010). Instructions were, "To what extent have you experienced the following in the PAST TWO WEEKS?" A sample item is, "Had tension in various muscles." Responses were made on a 5-point frequency scale (1 = Never to 5 = Constantly). In addition, client negative affect (α = .87) was assessed with five items (Thompson, 2007). Instructions were, "Thinking about yourself, over the PAST TWO WEEKS to what extent did you generally feel:" A sample item is, "Afraid." Responses were made on a 5-point frequency scale (1 = Never to 5 = Always).

paragraphs: Table 1 reports descriptive statistics. In our analysis sample, 44.6% reported engaging in at least some client incivility during one of their recent sessions. Structural equation modeling was used to examine Research Question 1. Items were used as latent factor indicators for all constructs but client incivility; given our sample size, to reduce model complexity, we used an item parceling approach (Little et al., 2002) for client incivility (four parcels as indicators, with three items randomly assigned to each parcel). As depicted in Figure 1, self-reported client incivility was regressed onto the predictors and control variables (the four exogenous This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

variables were set free to correlate). The model fit the data well, χ 2 (242) = 474.59, p < .001, CFI = .93, RMSEA = .07 (90% C.I. = .06-.08).foot_1 Although provider satisfaction did not explain incremental variability in client incivility (β = .00, p = .99), clients were more likely to report engaging in client incivility when they perceived their provider as not actively engaged (i.e., involved) in positive information exchange about the treatment process (β = -.65, p < .001) even after accounting for client negative affect (β = -.01, p = .91) and somatic strain (β = .22, p = .04). Given the nature of our design, such that we had self-report cross-sectional data wherein commonmethod variance may be a potential issue (Podsakoff et al., 2012), true causality cannot be established, however, Study 1 results provide preliminary support for Research Question 1; client incivility appears to convey discontent with the quality of care to providers, specifically the providers' involvement in client interactions.

sections.paper_id: Matthews et al. - 2022 - Can incivility be informative Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.
paragraphs: Study 1 results help to establish that client incivility may signal client discontent with the quality of care by providers, consistent with our conceptualization of client incivility as a kind of need signal from clients to providers. In Study 2, we examined client incivility from the providers' perspective to assess whether providers experience client incivility, which taps into the observability of client incivility as a signal (i.e., signal observability; Connelly et al., 2011). Given that client discontent is an unobservable quality, client incivility (as a signal of this discontent) must be observable by the employee/provider for it to exert any impact. In addition, we examined the degree to which providers pay attention to client incivility and may regard client incivility as a meaningful and useful signal (i.e., receiver attention; Connelly et al., 2011). Ultimately, our theorized relation between client incivility and provider creativity should only hold if providers experience client incivility and perceive it as a meaningful signal.
Research Question 2: Do providers report experiencing client incivility, and attend to client incivility as a meaningful signal?

paragraphs: Email addresses for occupational therapists were obtained from a state licensing board. Participants were emailed and asked to complete a short survey. There was no participation incentive. Invitations were sent to 8,273foot_2 emails of which 3,663 were opened; 635 respondents participated (raw response = 7.7%, corrected response rate based on invitations opened = 17.3%). Of the participants, 191 did not finish the survey, and another 28 had limited contact with clients (i.e., they did not have active client caseloads); these respondents were excluded, resulting in an analysis sample of 416. The analysis sample was 90.2% female, and had a mean age of 44.00 (SD = 11.83) years. In terms of working environment, 24.5% of respondents reported working in an outpatient environment, 22.6% worked in inpatient short-term care, 12.7% worked in a community setting, 7.0% worked in inpatient longterm care, 5.3% worked at a private organization, and 23.1% worked in some other environment. The sample was predominately Caucasian (74.8%), with a mean field tenure of 16.15 years (SD = 11.27), and worked an average of 37.07 hr per week (SD = 9.86).

paragraphs: Several close-and open-ended questions were used to assess receiver attention to client incivility. They were administered after participants completed the client incivility measure and regardless of whether they had experienced client incivility. Participants were asked: "To what extent do you think there is value in paying attention when a client engages in these types of behaviors?" (1 = To a very small extent to 5 = To a very large extent), after which participants were presented with the open-ended prompt "Can you explain why you feel that way?." Providers were then asked,foot_3 "When clients engage in these rude and inconsiderate behaviors, how likely are the behaviors to provide useful information that you can use to guide the treatment plans you engage in?" (1 = Extremely unlikely to 4 = Extremely likely). Participants were also asked the open-ended question, "What do you do when a client engages in these types of rude and inconsiderate behaviors?" to further gauge the meaning and utility providers ascribe to client incivility.

paragraphs: Descriptive statistics were used to analyze all close-ended responses. Client incivility is pervasive; 79.1% of providers reported experiencing client incivility in the past two weeks. Specifically, 57.9% of participants endorsed the statement that clients had, "Paid little attention to your statements or showed little interest in your opinions" and 54.3% endorsed the item, "Interrupted or "spoke over" you." Respondents working in inpatient long-term care reported the highest level of client incivility (M = 1.98, SD = .97) whereas respondents working in an outpatient environment reported the lowest client incivility (M = 1.31, SD = .44).

Although we see evidence that client incivility was experienced relatively frequently by providers, which suggests that incivility is an observable signal, this data alone does not address whether providers perceive that client incivility contains meaningful and useful information, which relates to the issue of receiver attention. Results showed that 68.3% of providers responded "To a large extent" or "To a very large extent" that they think there is value in paying attention when a client engages in incivility, while another 23.2% indicated "To a moderate extent." In addition, 79.8% of providers indicated that when clients engage in uncivil behavior, those behaviors are "likely" or "extremely likely" to provide useful information that could be used to guide their treatment plans.

In turn, we analyzed the open-ended responses using topic modeling with the topicApp developed by Banks et al. (2018) and leveraged their guidance in preparing for and conducting the analyses. Topic modeling is an unsupervised approach to qualitative text analysis akin to factor analysis because researchers can identify topics (i.e., factors) that emerge from text (Banks et al., 2018). Human inference is required to interpret and label emergent topics, relying on output such as the important/frequently occurring words per topic, word clouds for each topic, and representative text for topics. Because both open-ended questions dealt with perceptions of client incivility, similar to Banks et al., we combined responses for each participant to increase the length of each document (i.e., response). We removed participants if they did not respond to a question, leaving n = 373 for analysis. The topicApp handles all required preprocessing of data (e.g., tokenization, cleaning). Banks et al. encourage parsimony by suggesting that researchers start by exploring fewer topics and increase the number of topics if there is value added. We ran several models by varying the parameters (e.g., number of topics, sparse terms, unigrams/bigrams, stemming; cf. Banks et al., 2018). We did not detect a meaningful difference between the three and two topic solutions, so we retained the latter for interpretation (Table 2).

We labeled the first topic Attending to the signal, because this topic dealt with how providers attend to client incivility. When client incivility occurs, providers infer that there is a reason for the incivility, and it is up to them to find out why it is occurring. Frequently occurring words for the topic included "feel," "listen," and "understand," which suggest that providers sincerely attend to client incivility to understand why it is happening. We labeled the second topic Acting on the signal, because this topic captured how providers respond to client incivility. Not only do providers attend to incivility, they appear to see it as meaningful and useful for informing treatment plans. Frequently occurring words for this topic included "need," "therapi" (stemmed), and "address," which suggest that providers use incivility to extract information that they can use to improve treatment. One emblematic quote read, in part, that "If the patient is expressing a concern, this information is useful to guide treatment plans and goals." To summarize, Study 2 shows that providers experience client incivility frequently and see client incivility as instrumental. Client incivility conveys useful information about the quality of their care, and the potential need to change treatment plans, which further supports our conceptualization of client incivility as a need signal (Connelly et al., 2011).

sections.paper_id: Matthews et al. - 2022 - Can incivility be informative Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.
paragraphs: Study 1 supports clients' incivility as a signal of discontent, and Study 2 supports the observability and importance of such signals to providers, the receivers. Based on the qualitative results within Study 2, it seems that providers go through a process of receiver calibration (Branzei et al., 2004) in which they assign meaning to signals that should inform an eventual response. Still, neither study explicitly links experiences of client incivility to the response of interest here, provider creativity, nor do they address the signaling timeline. Thus, we turn our attention to a nuanced theory of the temporal relationship between client incivility and creativity to test hypotheses regarding the client incivility-provider creativity signaling timeline.

sections.paper_id: Matthews et al. - 2022 - Can incivility be informative Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.
paragraphs: Topic label Treatment should be client-centered this way, that the therapist has a dialectal relationship with meeting the patient where they are at and encouraging higher performance, listening and responding while providing a therapeutic avenue for recovery.

Listen or redirect, context dependent. If the patient is expressing a concern, this information is useful to guide treatment plans and goals, if the patient is appears to be regularly rude and inconsiderate but willing to participate, than redirection. If the patient is however, rude and inconsiderate and not willing to participate, than I respect the patients opposition to participation, I see if there is anything I can do to encourage participation, and if not that I cancel treatment. Note. Model parameters: 2 topics, maximum 200 iterations, 15 documents minimum for terms, stemming, unigrams. Stop words include: behavior, behaviors, can, person, get, client, patient, time, sometime, sometimes, may, work.

That is, most constructs have a set-point (i.e., dynamic equilibrium theory; Headey, 2006) or baseline that is stable over time. However, the same construct also demonstrates within-occasion variability (Hertzog & Nesselroade, 1987). Aquino and Lamertz (2004) capture these differences by distinguishing between episodic (variant) and institutionalized (invariant) workplace victimization, terms which we utilize. Existing research points to experienced incivility having both episodic and institutionalized components. For example, Matthews and Ritter (2019) reported 1-month test-retest correlations for incivility, ranging from .62 to .69. These cross-lagged correlations suggest that people exposed to incivility are likely to be exposed to incivility in the future, indicating that some incivility is chronic or institutionalized (Matthews & Ritter, 2019). That said, if incivility was entirely stable and there was no episodic variability in incivility, then the autocorrelations would approach one. Thus, employees seem to experience a typical or baseline level of incivility that is stable over time, with some temporal variability or fluctuation also present as specific incivility episodes occur. Specific to the present research, our results suggest that clients enact (Study 1) and providers experience (Study 2) some level of institutionalized client incivility. Some providers, by the nature of their work and/or clients (Study 2), may regularly experience higher levels of incivility (Riskin et al., 2017). Providers who experience higher levels of institutionalized client incivility likely see these experiences, "as a predictable and inevitable part of their relationship" with clients (Aquino & Lamertz, 2004, p. 1027). Yet institutionalized client incivility does not preclude providers from having episodic experiences of client incivility that deviate from the norm. Institutionalized incivility captures the nature of the overall relationship between the provider and their clients, whereas episodic client incivility reflects discrete instances which deviate from the well-established pattern of client behavior. As such, episodic client incivility reflects the ebb-and-flow of client incivility over time.

Creativity is also separable into institutionalized and episodic components (e.g., Gilson & Madjar, 2011), although this was not always appreciated. Historically, creativity was conceptualized in terms of the "creative person" and seen as stable (i.e., trait-like; Torrance, 1972). Scholars have examined numerous constructs thought to influence this invariant component of creativity (e.g., personality, Feist, 1998). However, over time, the importance of situation and time variant constructs has emerged (van Knippenberg & Hirst, 2015). Scholars have considered, for example, support (Madjar et al., 2011) and time pressure (Baer & Oldham, 2006) in relation to the temporal ebb-and-flow of creativity. Thus, it appears that both institutionalized and episodic components to creativity exist (e.g., Amabile, 1996;van Knippenberg & Hirst, 2015). We expect that the different temporal components of incivility and creativity may have different relationships with one another.

Despite our findings in Study 2, which suggest that providers see meaning and utility in client incivility, extant research shows clearly that incivility is an interpersonal stressor (Cortina et al., 2017). Accordingly, we expect that providers who experience high levels of institutionalized incivility should experience deleterious effects of that incivility. A representative quote in Table 2 suggests that providers may terminate the relationship if client incivility is frequent, supporting this expectation. Such destructive outcomes align with a variety of theoretical perspectives which suggest that incivility undercuts enactment of one's sense of self in their work (Chen et al., 2013) and the availability of resources (e.g., Sliter et al., 2012), both of which should reduce ability and motivation to perform creatively. Applying signaling theory, institutionalized incivility reflects high signal frequency (Janney & Folta, 2003). Frequent, consistent signals more effectively reduce information asymmetry than do infrequent or conflicting signals (Park & Mezias, 2005). Institutionalized incivility provides a frequent, consistent signal of client discontent with provider performance that may be demotivating for the provider. Indeed, research by Cortina and Magley (2009) demonstrates that persistent workplace incivility tends to lead to employees' avoidance of the uncivil relationship, counter to the investment of time and energy needed for creativity (Amabile & Pratt, 2016). Overall, consistent with signaling theory and existing research (e.g., Porath & Erez, 2007, 2009;Yip et al., 2018), institutionalized client incivility likely creates a context wherein providers cannot maintain systematically high levels of creativity.
Hypothesis 1: The institutionalized component of client incivility has a negative relationship with the institutionalized component of provider creativity.

We expect a similar pattern, concurrently, when episodic experiences of incivility are considered. Consistent with traditional perspectives that incivility is an interpersonal stressor (Cortina et al., 2017), if providers experience an uncharacteristic spike in client incivility, such incivility should relate to lower creativity at that time, as indexed by episodic creativity. For instance, a client's rude comment may make the provider hesitant to recommend a new strengthening exercise at that time. Cortina and Magley's (2009) research does in fact demonstrate that uncharacteristic instances of incivility concurrently relate to detachment from the situation which, again, should undermine creativity (Amabile & Pratt, 2016). This hypothesis is consistent with research showing a negative concurrent incivility-creativity relation (Porath & Erez, 2007, 2009;Yip et al., 2018).
Hypothesis 2: The episodic component of client incivility has a negative concurrent relationship with the episodic component of provider creativity.

It is when we consider the larger signaling timeline that we predict that episodic experiences of client incivility have instrumental utility, which is suggested in part by results from providers in Study 2. Episodic incivility is an infrequent, inconsistent signal as it differs from the typical level of incivility in the client/provider relationship. Inconsistent signals do not completely reduce information asymmetry (Park & Mezias, 2005), meaning the client has not repeatedly and undeniably indicated discontent with employee performance. As such, although uncharacteristic client incivility may be demotivating and result in employee detachment concurrently (Cortina & Magley, 2009), it is possible that an eventual instrumental outcome could result given that the inconsistent signal does not indicate an insurmountable level of discontent. Consistent with signaling theory, we suggest that providers need time to reflect on instances of incivility, and for the information to impact provider creativity (i.e., to seek out new/alternative way to implement treatment plans). In line with the creativity literature's understanding of incubation (Madjar & Shalley, 2008), it may also take time for providers to develop a creative change to their approach. Thus, for example, although rude remarks may inhibit provider creativity at This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.
This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly.

that time, following reflection, the provider may creatively modify their treatment plans;foot_4 providers may interpret client incivility as a signal that a need for change exists, and over time, adjust and apply more creative solutions to their treatment plans.
Hypothesis 3: The episodic component of client incivility has a positive lagged relationship with the episodic component of provider creativity.

Additional relations may exist when studying client incivility and provider creativity over time in the context of signaling theory and provider/client relationships. Broadly, signaling theory suggests that signals can be reinforced when a receiver notices and reacts to them, in a process called countersignaling (Connelly et al., 2011). For instance, in sequential bargaining contexts, countersignals sent from receivers may in turn result in more efficient signaling from signalers (Srivastava, 2001). Information asymmetry can therefore be considered bidirectional in that (a) receivers look for useful information regarding signalers' qualities but (b) that signalers also look for information about the reception and reactions to their signals to improve the effectiveness of future signals (Connelly et al., 2011). The resulting implication that signaling is an iterative process is especially relevant to a client/provider context in which client pain and discomfort repeatedly arise throughout the challenging process of rehabilitation and in which mutual provider and client input is therefore "basic" to care provision (Neistadt, 1995;p. 429).

Applied here, episodic creativity by providers may also serve as a countersignal back to clients in that it may reinforce incivility as an effective signal of discontent (i.e., one that sparks novel provider attempts to improve care). It is important to note that a client may react positively or negatively, civilly or uncivilly, to a providers' episodic creativity in the moment, depending on the perceived effectiveness of the provider response; this simply represents a response to the receiver's signal reaction, not countersignaling. Instead, countersignaling would involve provider creativity positively predicting future incivility, as a seemingly effective signal of any future discontent that naturally arises as part of the difficult rehabilitation process. In total, if a provider changes a treatment plan after uncivil behavior from clients (i.e., the initial signal), that change in care signals back to clients (i.e., countersignals) that being uncivil results in behavior change by the provider and, in turn, reinforces future incivility. Thus, we expect that providers' episodic creativity positively predicts client incivility 2 weeks later.
Hypothesis 4: The episodic component of provider creativity has a positive lagged relationship with the episodic component of client incivility.

We expect a similar pattern, concurrently, when episodic experiences of incivility are considered. Consistent with traditional perspectives that incivility is an interpersonal stressor (Cortina et al., 2017), if providers experience an uncharacteristic spike in client incivility, such incivility should relate to lower creativity at that time, as indexed by episodic creativity. For instance, a client's rude comment may make the provider hesitant to recommend a new strengthening exercise at that time. Cortina and Magley's (2009) research does in fact demonstrate that uncharacteristic instances of incivility concurrently relate to detachment from the situation which, again, should undermine creativity (Amabile & Pratt, 2016). This hypothesis is consistent with research showing a negative concurrent incivility-creativity relation (Porath & Erez, 2007, 2009;Yip et al., 2018).

Hypothesis 2: The episodic component of client incivility has a negative concurrent relationship with the episodic component of provider creativity.

It is when we consider the larger signaling timeline that we predict that episodic experiences of client incivility have instrumental utility, which is suggested in part by results from providers in Study 2. Episodic incivility is an infrequent, inconsistent signal as it differs from the typical level of incivility in the client/provider relationship. Inconsistent signals do not completely reduce information asymmetry (Park & Mezias, 2005), meaning the client has not repeatedly and undeniably indicated discontent with employee performance. As such, although uncharacteristic client incivility may be demotivating and result in employee detachment concurrently (Cortina & Magley, 2009), it is possible that an eventual instrumental outcome could result given that the inconsistent signal does not indicate an insurmountable level of discontent. Consistent with signaling theory, we suggest that providers need time to reflect on instances of incivility, and for the information to impact provider creativity (i.e., to seek out new/alternative way to implement treatment plans). In line with the creativity literature's understanding of incubation (Madjar & Shalley, 2008), it may also take time for providers to develop a creative change to their approach. Thus, for example, although rude remarks may inhibit provider creativity at This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

that time, following reflection, the provider may creatively modify their treatment plans;foot_4 providers may interpret client incivility as a signal that a need for change exists, and over time, adjust and apply more creative solutions to their treatment plans.

Hypothesis 3: The episodic component of client incivility has a positive lagged relationship with the episodic component of provider creativity.

Additional relations may exist when studying client incivility and provider creativity over time in the context of signaling theory and provider/client relationships. Broadly, signaling theory suggests that signals can be reinforced when a receiver notices and reacts to them, in a process called countersignaling (Connelly et al., 2011). For instance, in sequential bargaining contexts, countersignals sent from receivers may in turn result in more efficient signaling from signalers (Srivastava, 2001). Information asymmetry can therefore be considered bidirectional in that (a) receivers look for useful information regarding signalers' qualities but (b) that signalers also look for information about the reception and reactions to their signals to improve the effectiveness of future signals (Connelly et al., 2011). The resulting implication that signaling is an iterative process is especially relevant to a client/provider context in which client pain and discomfort repeatedly arise throughout the challenging process of rehabilitation and in which mutual provider and client input is therefore "basic" to care provision (Neistadt, 1995;p. 429).

Applied here, episodic creativity by providers may also serve as a countersignal back to clients in that it may reinforce incivility as an effective signal of discontent (i.e., one that sparks novel provider attempts to improve care). It is important to note that a client may react positively or negatively, civilly or uncivilly, to a providers' episodic creativity in the moment, depending on the perceived effectiveness of the provider response; this simply represents a response to the receiver's signal reaction, not countersignaling. Instead, countersignaling would involve provider creativity positively predicting future incivility, as a seemingly effective signal of any future discontent that naturally arises as part of the difficult rehabilitation process. In total, if a provider changes a treatment plan after uncivil behavior from clients (i.e., the initial signal), that change in care signals back to clients (i.e., countersignals) that being uncivil results in behavior change by the provider and, in turn, reinforces future incivility. Thus, we expect that providers' episodic creativity positively predicts client incivility 2 weeks later.

Hypothesis 4: The episodic component of provider creativity has a positive lagged relationship with the episodic component of client incivility.

That is, most constructs have a set-point (i.e., dynamic equilibrium theory; Headey, 2006) or baseline that is stable over time. However, the same construct also demonstrates within-occasion variability (Hertzog & Nesselroade, 1987). Aquino and Lamertz (2004) capture these differences by distinguishing between episodic (variant) and institutionalized (invariant) workplace victimization, terms which we utilize. Existing research points to experienced incivility having both episodic and institutionalized components. For example, Matthews and Ritter (2019) reported 1-month test-retest correlations for incivility, ranging from .62 to .69. These cross-lagged correlations suggest that people exposed to incivility are likely to be exposed to incivility in the future, indicating that some incivility is chronic or institutionalized (Matthews & Ritter, 2019). That said, if incivility was entirely stable and there was no episodic variability in incivility, then the autocorrelations would approach one. Thus, employees seem to experience a typical or baseline level of incivility that is stable over time, with some temporal variability or fluctuation also present as specific incivility episodes occur. Specific to the present research, our results suggest that clients enact (Study 1) and providers experience (Study 2) some level of institutionalized client incivility. Some providers, by the nature of their work and/or clients (Study 2), may regularly experience higher levels of incivility (Riskin et al., 2017). Yet institutionalized client incivility does not preclude providers from having episodic experiences of client incivility that deviate from the norm. Institutionalized incivility captures the nature of the overall relationship between the provider and their clients, whereas episodic client incivility reflects discrete instances which deviate from the well-established pattern of client behavior. As such, episodic client incivility reflects the ebb-and-flow of client incivility over time.

Historically, creativity was conceptualized in terms of the "creative person" and seen as stable (i.e., trait-like; Torrance, 1972). Scholars have examined numerous constructs thought to influence this invariant component of creativity (e.g., personality, Feist, 1998). However, over time, the importance of situation and time variant constructs has emerged (van Knippenberg & Hirst, 2015). Scholars have considered, for example, support (Madjar et al., 2011) and time pressure (Baer & Oldham, 2006) in relation to the temporal ebb-and-flow of creativity. sections.header_raw: Exploring Differences in Incremental and Radical Creativity
sections.header_norm: exploring differences in incremental and radical creativity
paragraphs: Just as the incivility literature continues to develop in an effort to address conflicting research results and incorporates new streams of research and theory (Cortina et al., 2017;Miner et al., 2018), so too does the creativity literature. Researchers have frequently reported on contradictory results, confusing our understanding of creativity's nomological network. As an example, researchers have debated the relationship between affect and creativity for years (Baas et al., 2008), with some authors arguing that positive affect leads to creativity (e.g., Amabile et al., 2005) and others arguing that negative affect leads to creativity (e.g., George & Zhou, 2002). Researchers on both sides of the argument have theorized and empirically tested their opposing hypotheses, finding support for their models and nonsignificant effects for the opposite argument.

Long-standing debates, like that for affect-creativity, have led scholars to reexamine the original definition of creativity, such that scholars have argued that the unitary view of creativity is inappropriate for assessing such a complex construct (Gilson et al., 2012;Sternberg, 1999). An evolving argument is that creativity cannot be understood using one construct, and our definitions need to be further delineated to truly understand creativity's nomological network. An emerging distinction is between incremental (i.e., small changes to existing processes) and radical creativity (i.e., new or revolutionary ideas; Gilson & Madjar, 2011). In a health care context, for example, incremental creativity may manifest when a provider working to improve clients' bilateral coordination (i.e., ability to control both sides of the body) substitutes colored blocks for wooden blocks because the provider finds that their clients are dissatisfied with plain wooden blocks. Alternatively, radical provider creativity could include the same provider trying something completely different, like working with clients with percussion instruments to improve their bilateral coordination. Research suggests that incremental and radical creativity are distinct and may have different predictors (Jaussi & Randel, 2014).

Relevant to the present study, incremental and radical creativity relate differently to positive social factors in the workplace (i.e., coworker trust; Xu et al., 2018). Although this suggests that incivility, as a negative social factor in the workplace, may also result in differential associations with incremental and radical creativity, to our knowledge this has not been explicitly tested. Additionally, radical creativity is thought to require "shelter" (i.e., some level of concealment and time) to develop whereas incremental creativity may develop through application (Gilson & Litchfield, 2017, p. 82). As such, the role of time may be different across the two creativity types. We therefore also explored whether hypothesized relations with client incivility differed by creativity type.
Research Question 3: Do the relations between client incivility and provider creativity differ by creativity type?

paragraphs: We targeted a large sample of providers who reported their experiences of client incivility and (incremental and radical) creativity in client care via a series of 10-min electronic surveys every 2 weeks across five assessment points. Similar to Study 2, email addresses for occupational therapists and assistants were obtained from two state licensing boards; these licensing boards were different from the one accessed in Study 2. weeks. Respondents were entered into a drawing for one of twenty $20 online gift certificates for each survey completed. Invitations were sent to 11,962foot_5 emails; 6,041 were opened. The Time 1 survey had 1,403 respondents (raw response = 11.7%, corrected response rate (based on invitations opened) = 23.2%); 201 failed to finish the survey. The remaining 1,182 respondents were invited to participate 2 weeks later at Time 2 with reminders sent 3 days later; 772 individuals participated at Time 2. The remaining surveys followed the same pattern with 608 respondents at Time 3, 472 at Time 4, and 431 at Time 5.

Respondents were retained if they completed three or more surveys and had an active case load, resulting in a sample of 503 which was 92.4% female and had a mean age of 41.89 (SD = 11.43) years. The sample was predominately Caucasian (92.4%) with a mean tenure of 6.77 years (SD = 5.56), and worked an average 38.27 hr per week (SD = 8.49).

sections.paper_id: Matthews et al. - 2022 - Can incivility be informative Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.
paragraphs: Client incivility was assessed using Matthews and Ritter's (2016) four-item scale, which was adapted from Cortina et al. (2013) for longitudinal research. Participants were asked to think about their clients when responding. A sample is, "Interrupted or "spoke over" you." Responses were made on a 5-point frequency scale (1 = Never to 5 = Many times). Again, as noted previously, Connelly et al. (2011) highlight that if receivers make an effective decision based on a signal, they are likely to attend to similar signals over time, which may originate from different signalers. Thus, we allowed providers to reflect on all clients because, in reality, they may experience multiple signals (incivility) from multiple signalers (clients).

sections.paper_id: Matthews et al. - 2022 - Can incivility be informative Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.
paragraphs: Creativity items were adapted from existing scales to measure incremental and radical provider creativity (Gilson & Madjar, 2011;Madjar et al., 2011). Three items assessed incremental creativity (e.g., "I was very good at adapting already existing ideas to better meet my clients' needs") and four items measured radical creativity ("I developed truly radically new ways for meeting the needs of my clients"). Items were piloted in a sample of 650 social workers; social workers were selected given the critical role creativity plays in the profession (e.g., River et al., 2017).

Maximum likelihood confirmatory factor analyses, based on Time 1 analysis sample data only, suggested that a two-factor model, χ 2 (13) = 22.04, p = .06, CFI = .99, RMSEA = .03, fit better than a single-factor model, χ 2 (14) = 586.40, p < .001, CFI = .75, RMSEA = .25; Δχ 2 (1) = 564.36, p < .01; the factors correlated at .55 ( p < .01) with standardized loadings ≥.77. To establish construct validity, we examined how the measures related to relevant correlates identified based on the literature (e.g., Amabile & Pratt, 2016). As expected, the measures systematically correlated with intrinsic motivation (r incremental = .37, r radical = .40, p < .01; Tremblay et al., 2009), autonomy (r incremental = .23, r radical = .19, p < .01; Morgeson & Humphrey, 2006), and job routinization (r incremental = -.22, r radical = -.18, p < .01; Melamed et al., 1995).

sections.paper_id: Matthews et al. - 2022 - Can incivility be informative Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.
paragraphs: We used trait-state-occasion (TSO) modelingfoot_6 (AMOS; Arbuckle, 2012), following procedures recommended by Cole et al. (2005) and Cole (2012). In this approach, latent time variant factors are extracted based on multiple indicators at each time point, which are decomposed into variance that is a function of a time invariant factor and occasion-specific factor. The occasion-specific factor represents potential occasion-related conditions that affect the time variant factor beyond the time invariant factor. In turn, the occasion-specific factors may demonstrate systematic persistence over time, which is modeled as an autoregressive effect. We applied a correlated uniqueness approach to control for shared method variance (Marsh, 1993); item error terms, across data waves, correlated freely (LaGrange & Cole, 2008).

Given our interest in how the different components of client incivility and provider creativity relate to one another, multistatedouble trait models (Steyer et al., 2015) were computed. We analyzed both client incivility and provider creativity simultaneously, with a separate set of models for each type of creativity (i.e., radical and incremental). To test Hypothesis 1, the institutionalized (i.e., time-invariant) client incivility component was set free to correlate with the institutionalized creativity component. To test Hypothesis 2, the within-wave occasion-specific (i.e., episodic) factors were set free to correlate across client incivility and provider creativity. Finally, to test Hypotheses 3 and 4, we included cross-lag path estimates between episodic client incivility and creativity (e.g., Time 1 episodic client incivility was set to predict Time 2 episodic creativity; Time 1 episodic creativity was set to predict Time 2 This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly. episodic client incivility). To ensure a conservative test of the model, the loadings for the indicator items were constrained to be equal to their counterparts across waves (within each construct), and indicator intercepts were constrained to be equal to their counterparts across waves (within each construct; Geiser et al., 2015). In addition, the occasion error variances and the stability effects were constrained (Cole, 2012), as were the correlations between the within-wave occasion-specific factors, and the cross-lagged path estimates (Steyer et al., 2015).

sections.paper_id: Matthews et al. - 2022 - Can incivility be informative Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.
paragraphs: Table 3 includes descriptive statistics. Across the five time points, 78.3% of providers experienced client incivility. Table 4 reports statistics relevant to interpreting the TSO models. The client incivilityincremental creativity model fit the data well, χ 2 (538) = 1147.18, p < .001, CFI = .94, RMSEA = .05. As reported in Table 4, 66% of the variance in client incivility over the fives waves was a function of the institutionalized nature of the construct and 34% was a function of the episodic component. For incremental creativity, 48% of the variance was institutionalized and 52% was episodic. Contrary to Hypotheses 1 and 2, institutionalized client incivility was not related to the institutionalized incremental creativity component (r = -.11, p = .08), nor was the concurrent relationship between episodic client incivility and episodic incremental creativity (r = .02, p = .72). However, in support of Hypothesis 3, episodic client incivility had a positive lagged relationship with the episodic component of incremental creativity (β = .11, p = .008). We saw limited evidence of countersignaling (β = .09, p = .07), thus Hypothesis 4 was not supported.

The client incivility-radical creativity model also fit the data well, χ 2 (716) = 1209.71, p < .01, CFI = .96, RMSEA = .04. As reported in Table 4, 65% of the variance in client incivility was institutionalized and 35% was episodic. For radical creativity, 69% of the variance was institutionalized and 31% was episodic. Contrary to Hypothesis 1, institutionalized client incivility was not related to invariant radical creativity (r = -.02, p = .75). Although the concurrent relationship between episodic client incivility and episodic radical creativity was significant (r = .10, p = .02), it was in the opposite direction, so Hypothesis 2 was not supported. Hypothesis 3 was again supported; episodic client incivility had a positive lagged relationship with the episodic component of radical creativity (β = .09, p = .04). We also found support for Hypothesis 4; episodic radical creativity had a positive lagged relationship with the episodic component of client incivility (β = .15, p = .005).

sections.paper_id: Matthews et al. - 2022 - Can incivility be informative Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.
paragraphs: In Research Question 3, we considered the possibility that the proposed relationships between client incivility and provider creativity differ as a function of creativity type. On the one hand, we do see some consistency in our findings. Hypothesis 1 was not supported for either type of creativity as there was no systematic relationship between institutionalized incivility and the invariant component of provider creativity. Alternatively, Hypothesis 3 was supported for both types of creativity; episodic incivility was associated with more incremental and radical creativity in the future. However, as it relates to Hypotheses 2 and 4, we saw systematic differences. Although Hypothesis 2 was not supported for either type of creativity, we observed a significant positive correlation between episodic incivility and episodic radical creativity (i.e., in the opposite direction predicted); changes in episodic incivility resulted in the concurrent (increased) changes in engagement in radical creativity. In turn, we observed support for countersignaling (Hypothesis 4) with radical creativity but not for incremental creativity. Consequently, there are both similarities and meaningful differences in results as a function of provider creativity type. We expand on these differences in our general discussion.

paragraphs: Scholars agree that the study of workplace incivility needs sound theoretical guidance, although they differ on the path forward. Schilpzand et al. (2016) criticized the incivility literature due to the lack of theoretical development. They suggest that future research may benefit from a unified framework, building from such theories as the transactional model of stress and coping (Lazarus & Folkman, 1984). But drawing upon such approaches has resulted in an assumption by many scholars that incivility is universally deleterious, which Miner et al. (2018) identified is a potentially erroneous assumption that may meaningfully impede progress.

Our program of research was shaped by signaling theory, a theory which has been applied only recently to study workplace mistreatment (Lindsey et al., 2017). Our results suggest that incivility from clients signals important information to providers, information which they may use to provide more creative client care over time. Results from Study 1 show that incivility is an "honest" signal (i.e., feedback; Kim & Kim, 2020) from clients that changes in care are needed. Results from Study 2 suggest that providers regularly experience incivility from their clients, and that they view client incivility as providing valuable information which can be used to provide better care. Finally, Study 3 provides the most comprehensive evidence for a client-provider signaling timeline. Providers appear to respond to client incivility via increased creativity, which in the case of episodic radical creativity may even act as a countersignal that reinforces client's uncivil behaviors. These findings suggest that signaling theory may hold considerable value for uncovering new insights into incivility as an efficacious signal and should be utilized in future incivility research.

Our results change the conversation around incivility and creativity in several ways. In particular, we provide evidence that incivility may lead to greater creativity in specific relationships and contexts. We found consistent evidence for lagged effects, such that higher-than-usual levels of incivility from organizational outsiders were linked to greater incremental and radical employee creativity over time. In line with signaling theory predictions, these lagged effects suggest that the signaling utility of outsider incivility may be delayed, as employees take time to interpret the message and make modifications. Future research utilizing signaling theory may delve into the cognitive processes that may explain these relationships. For example, customer mistreatment can lead service This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

employees to ruminate on the experience (Wang et al., 2013). Providers may ruminate on the incivility, which may drive them to develop more creative interventions for their clients. Vahle-Hinz et al. (2017) found that one type of reflection-problem-solving pondering-related positively to work creativity. Our Study 2 results align with this prior finding, since providers appear focused on understanding, learning from, and resolving problems from which client incivility originates.

In addition, results showed that when clients engaged in higher levels of incivility than normal, providers were more likely to engage in radical creativity at that time. This finding may clarify contradictory results from past research. Specifically, negative feedback can enhance novelty, but interpersonal trust is needed for creative decision outcomes (Ford & Gioia, 2000). It may be that negative information such as client incivility contributes to both novel and useful outcomes (i.e., overall creative performance) when delivered in an otherwise civil, trusting relationship.

Our study also has implications for understanding the precursors to incivility, a focus called for by Schilpzand et al. (2016). Based on the notion of countersignaling (Connelly et al., 2011), we examined whether provider creativity may drive client incivility over time. Although no relation was observed for incremental creativity, we did find that when providers were radically creative (in response to experienced incivility), they also experienced higher levels of client incivility over time. This finding suggests that if a provider engages in creative changes when clients engage in uncivil behavior, a relatively strong signal (i.e., radical creativity) is needed to sufficiently signal back to clients that being uncivil results in provider behavior change. That is, minor tweaks to treatment plans were not sufficient to signal back to clients.

Still, an alternative explanation for these findings should be considered. Indeed, client incivility as a response to provider creativity may be due to the reinforcement of that incivility via better, more creative care (i.e., a signal and countersignal); however, radical creativity is also risky and has potential for negative repercussions (Madjar et al., 2011), meaning incivility responses could also indicate clients' disappointment in ineffective, risky new care strategies by the provider. Put another way, clients may resist radical changes in an ongoing care relationship, with negative reactions (i.e., incivility in the present study) strengthened when the change is deemed inappropriate (Kreitner, 1992). Within a social interactionist perspective, incivility may be the result of one party's desire to reciprocate undesired or provocative treatment from another party, stimulating a "tit for tat" spiral of mistreatment (Andersson & Pearson, 1999). In the context of a health care provider and client, it is possible that a tit-for-tat process unfolds between uncivil clients and radically creative providers. Specifically, the provider may reciprocate the client's incivility by changing their behavior (i.e., being more creative or engaging in riskier care strategies). The change in the provider's behavior may be perceived by the client as a reciprocal response prompting the desire to reciprocate again, creating a spiral. Because multiple theories can potentially be applied to explain this finding, future research should competitively test different incivility theories to better understand the processes underlying client incivility and provider responses.

Finally, we underline the notion that a unitary view of creativity may mask the underlying complexity of the construct (Gilson et al., 2012;Sternberg, 1999). Radical creativity is often driven by the problem itself, whereas incremental creativity is driven by solutionrelated thought (Gilson & Madjar, 2011). In this context, the signal of episodic client incivility may focus provider attention on the problem (e.g., client discontent with a therapy activity), leading to radical creativity in the short-term. However, over time, as immediacy of the problem fades and the provider weighs ideas that are both problem and solution-driven, either radical or incremental creativity may emerge, which may explain the positive lagged relationships observed for both types of creativity. Moreover, a key distinction between radical and incremental creativity is the novelty of radical creativity, such that radically creative treatment options are very different from current treatment. When providers are faced with higher-than-normal incivility, rather than slightly changing the treatment program (i.e., incremental creativity), they may take a more novel treatment plan in response to the incivility.
