sections.paper_id: Matthews et al. - 2022 - Can incivility be informative Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.
paragraphs: Our findings also have implications for practice. Like many health care professions, accredited therapy programs, including in This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly. Realistically, however, it is important that therapists be aware that not all clients will be, or can be, fully engaged and cooperative. Understanding that client incivility is unavoidable in certain cases (i.e., instituionalized), particularly when working with clients who are cognitiviely impaired or those suffering from residual disabilities resulting from brain damage, can help providers prepare for the challenges they may experience with such clients. Training may enable providers to be more aware of the various forms of incivility, and potentially enable providers to anticipate client frustration prior to their becoming uncivil. Training providers to remain empathetic may help them channel energy to more creative methods of addressing client incivility.

Additionally, awareness that client incivility is not only common, but potentially beneficial in determining the appropriate treatment, may help providers to better cope with and address client incivility. Given the findings from Study 3, it is crucial that strategies aimed at minimizing client incivility do so in a way that benefits provider well-being without reducing providers' creative performance. Making providers aware that client incivility in some cases may serve as motivation to engage in creative efforts to please the client, may assist providers in altering how they perceive client incivility. This is particularly important given that our findings suggest that when providers try radically new methods, clients may further engage in incivility. Thus, ensuring that providers anticipate continued client incivility when trying radically new methods may help providers employ adaptive methods to prevent the deleterious consequences that may otherwise occur as a result of repeated incivility (Schilpzand et al., 2016). To be clear, the positive relationship between incivility and creativity should not be taken as evidence that incivility should be encouraged. Rather, as previously noted, raising awareness among providers may assist them in preparing for the challenges and remaining focused and empathetic.

Organizations can take steps to help mitigate avoidable client incivility and offer providers support with unavoidable cases. Policies should incorporate definitions and context-specific examples of incivility and set expectations for civil client behavior. Additionally, although research has suggested that negative feedback can boost creativity (Ford & Gioia, 2000), findings indicate that such feedback is more effective for creative performance when delivered developmentally (Zhou, 2003). Thus, client intake programs could emphasize building trust between the client and provider to encourage honest, constructive communication of discontent rather than delivery of negative feedback via incivility. Furthermore, it is important that organizational leaders cultivate and nurture a supportive workplace environment that encourages providers to lean on one another This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

This article is intended solely for the personal use of the individual user and is not to be disseminated broadly. for support. Regular team meetings that allow providers to share experiences of client incivility may serve to enhance providers' well-being and gain constructive consultation from peers.

While the present study focused specifically on the clientprovider relationship nested in the context of health care, wherein most respondents were female (Gabriel et al., 2018), our findings may be generalizable to other relationships outside of the health care context. Employee/outsider relationships such as those found throughout the retail and service industry, contractors, and in certain cases even ex-patriots may experience incivility. Although a strong organizational culture that values and promotes acceptable behavior and norms may help curb incivility among organizational insiders (Estes & Wang, 2008), such efforts may not be enough to influence the behavior of outsiders. Despite organizations having well-defined behavioral rules that are embraced by insiders, outsiders may ignore them.

sections.header_raw: Limitations, Strengths, and Additional Research Directions
sections.header_norm: limitations strengths and additional research directions
paragraphs: Our studies are not without limitations. The primary limitation of our data is that it was self-reported from clients and providers. Capturing the perspective of both targets and perpetrators offers a holistic perspective that is often missing from mistreatment research (cf. Bowling & Beehr, 2006). However, future research could improve upon our methodology by capturing client and provider dyads to better understand intentions, perceptions, and responses to both incivility and creativity within client-provider relationships. This may be a challenge in the context of health care as existing client privacy laws (i.e., Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act) limit the feasibility of collecting matched clientprovider data. If achievable, though, dyadic data may also allow for more detailed analysis of information asymmetry. For instance, these designs would be equipped to more directly answer questions about signaler intent, receiver attention and interpretation, and any countersignaling responses from multiple perspectives regarding the same uncivil event, rather than incivility and creativity across various client relationships. The findings surrounding employee creativity should also be interpreted in the context of the self-report methods we used. There is currently no consensus on whether objective or subjective methods provide superior measurement of creativity in particular, but the two methods are moderately correlated (Park et al., 2016). The use of self-report methods is supported by arguments that employees themselves have the most direct insight into their own creativity (Shalley et al., 2009). Nonetheless, we suggest future researchers replicate and extend the present results with other reports and objective measurement as social desirability and illusory superiority may inflate self-report.

Relatedly, the cross-sectional nature of the data collected in Studies 1 and 2 limits causal interpretation of the findings. This limitation is particularly relevant to the interpretation of Study 1. Namely, the positive relation between clients' enacted incivility and perceptions of their providers' lack of involvement does clearly indicate that the two experiences co-occur, but this result could reflect incivility as a signal of discontent with uninvolved treatment, as proposed, or provider disengagement from rude clients. Fortunately, the longitudinal design in Study 3 provides some related answers to the questions posed by the Study 1 results. The bidirectional results indicate that client and provider behaviors likely reciprocally predict one another, which is echoed in health care research (Neistadt, 1995). In total, client incivility can reasonably be considered a signal of discontent with provider involvement, though the two likely feed into one another. Dyadic and event-based designs would again be helpful in more explicitly parsing out causality over the course of a client/provider relationship.

Another previously noted limitation of our sample is the overrepresentation of women, which accurately characterizes the occupational groups targeted but not the overall working population. Women report greater instances of incivility, and often greater harm, by incivility than do men (Yao et al., 2021;Zurbrügg & Miner, 2016). The instrumental potential of incivility is a newer development and, thus, gender differences in attending to, interpreting, and reacting to incivility as an indirect signal of discontent are not yet known. Given further evidence that men and women differentially react to other forms of feedback (e.g., Motro & Ellis, 2017), future research should similarly examine differential evaluations and responses to the informational component of incivility across gender.

Despite these limitations, our research benefits from several strengths. For example, as mentioned, we utilized a longitudinal design in Study 3, and temporal separation of measurement mitigates common method bias (Podsakoff et al., 2012). In addition, the sample sizes in Study 2 and Study 3 were large, which is important, especially in the context of Study 3 for ensuring the reliability of lagged effects (Ford et al., 2014). Finally, our use of both quantitative and qualitative methods, and collection of data from clients and providers helps to triangulate our findings. These strengths, in light of our contributions, help to balance out the limitations.

There are additional research directions worthy of pursuit. First, we focused solely on workplace incivility from clients as an instrumental signal without considering other plausible signals of client discontent. We should note that while not the primary focus of this program of research, it is possible that client incivility is a facet of the larger negative feedback process that exists in workplaces (e.g., Dahling et al., 2017) and may have incremental and/or synergistic effects on creativity (Kim & Kim, 2020). Further, given the financial consequences associated with poor clinical outcomes and client dissatisfaction (Agency for Healthcare Research & Quality, 2016;Hall et al., 2010), providers have an incentive to leverage all available information, direct or indirect, and from various channels, not just the client. Additional research is needed to disentangle incivility from other, more explicit forms of negative feedback, from both clients and other sources such as formal performance feedback.

Another related possibility to examine in future research, in addition to incivility, is feedback from clients that is delivered with civility and respect (i.e., workplace civility). In contrast with research on workplace incivility, the literature on workplace civility is less well-developed, with much of it centered around the study of civility norms (e.g., Walsh et al., 2012). Weingart et al. (2015) model of interpersonal conflict could be used to integrate currently disparate streams of research on civil and uncivil feedback to guide future research on the relative contributions of both types of client behavior on provider creativity.

Second, we see significant utility in the use of signaling theory for mistreatment research (Lindsey et al., 2017), but there may be value in supplementing signaling theory with stress theories to examine why providers may interpret incivility as a useful signal. For example, a challenge-hindrance framework may be especially useful This document is copyrighted by the American Psychological Association or one of its allied publishers.

in this regard as challenge appraisals have been linked to creativity in the past (Ohly & Fritz, 2010). Study 2 data suggest that providers are likely to interpret client incivility in positive ways, consistent with challenge appraisals. It is also important for future research to assess the extent to which our findings generalize to other types of mistreatment. For example, the ambiguity inherent to incivility may foster deep processing and boost creativity, whereas unmistakably negative events may reduce thorough, generative processing (cf. Forgas & George, 2001) and, potentially, creative performance.

sections.paper_id: Matthews et al. - 2022 - Can incivility be informative Client incivility as a signal for provider creativity.
paragraphs: Workplace incivility has widespread negative impacts (Hershcovis, 2011) that should not be understated or overlooked. Yet, as noted by Miner et al. (2018), "it may be the case that outcomes of incivility are negative in some situations but null or positive in others depending on the social context in which they occur" (p. 6). Our findings provide tentative evidence in support of this nuanced perspective on workplace incivility, in the context of ongoing client/provider relationships. Persistent incivility from clients did not relate to employee creativity. Yet more isolated incidences of incivility may contain information about client discontent with care that providers attend to in hopes of improving care and that, over time, spark creative approaches to treatment by providers. Unfortunately, though, when providers engage in more radical creativity, this could reinforce incivility as an effective method of eliciting a novel treatment response from providers and thus prompt future client incivility. Overall, our research adds to existing conceptualizations of incivility as a stressor (Cortina et al., 2017) to suggest incivility may be an admittedly unhealthy and indirect (Lim et al., 2008) means of communicating valuable information that employees can use to adaptively adjust client care.
